name: Apple — Honeycrisp
notes: Needs a pollinator partner (e.g. Fuji, Gala). Thin fruit for best size.

name: Apple — Fuji
notes: Low chill requirement. Good for warmer climates. Pollinates well with Honeycrisp.

name: Pear — Bartlett
notes: Fire blight susceptibility. Plant with Bosc or Anjou for pollination.

name: Asian Pear
notes: Crisp apple-like texture. More disease resistant than European pears.

name: Peach — Reliance
notes: One of the hardiest peaches. Self-fertile. Excellent for zones 4–6.

name: Peach — Elberta
notes: Classic freestone peach. Self-fertile. Great for canning and fresh eating.

name: Plum — Stanley
notes: Classic European prune-plum. Self-fertile and heavy bearing.

name: Plum — Methley
notes: Very low chill hours — great for the South and mild climates. Self-fertile.

name: Cherry — Montmorency (Tart)
notes: Most popular tart cherry. Very cold hardy. Self-fertile.

name: Cherry — Bing (Sweet)
notes: Classic sweet cherry. Needs a pollinator (Rainier, Stella). Sensitive to late frost.

name: Fig — Brown Turkey
notes: One of the most cold-tolerant figs. Can be container grown in colder zones. Self-fertile.

name: Persimmon — American
notes: Native to eastern US. Extremely tough — drought, clay, poor soils. Wildlife magnet. Fruit sweetens after frost.

name: Persimmon — Fuyu (Asian)
notes: Non-astringent — can eat firm off tree. Self-fertile. Very ornamental in fall.

name: Mulberry — Illinois Everbearing
notes: Incredibly productive. Fruit like blackberries. Birds love it. Extremely tough once established.

name: Pawpaw
notes: Largest native fruit in North America — custard texture. Needs 2+ plants for pollination. Understory tree.

name: Elderberry — American
notes: Native. Plant 2+ for best berry production. Berries must be cooked. Flowers edible raw. Incredible wildlife value.

name: Gooseberry — Pixwell
notes: Very cold hardy. Self-fertile. Thorny canes. Tart berries great for jam. Restricted in some states — check local rules.

name: Gooseberry — Invicta
notes: High-yielding white/green berry. Mildew resistant. Popular in UK and northern US.

name: Black Currant
notes: Extremely high vitamin C. Self-fertile. Restricted in some US states (white pine blister rust host) — verify locally.

name: Red Currant
notes: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruits. Self-fertile. Beautiful hanging clusters. Jelly and cordial.

name: Raspberry — Caroline (Everbearing)
notes: Fall-bearing. Self-fertile. Cut all canes to ground in late winter for single fall crop. Excellent flavor.

name: Raspberry — Heritage
notes: Reliable everbearing variety. Two crops per year. Cold hardy to zone 4. Classic red flavor.

name: Blackberry — Triple Crown
notes: Thornless, semi-erect. Large sweet berries. Disease resistant. Excellent fresh and for preserves.

name: Blackberry — Apache
notes: Thornless, upright canes. Very large berries. Great for southern climates.

name: Blueberry — Bluecrop (Highbush)
notes: Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5). Plant 2+ varieties for best yield. Spectacular fall color.

name: Blueberry — Climax (Rabbiteye)
notes: For the South — very heat tolerant. More drought resistant than highbush. Needs Tifblue or Premier for pollination.

name: Strawberry — Ozark Beauty
notes: Everbearing. Named for the Ozarks — great for midwest and mid-South. Runners spread readily.

name: Hazelnut — American
notes: Native to eastern US. Excellent wildlife food. Drought tolerant once established. Plant 2+ for nut production.

name: Hazelnut — European / Filbert
notes: Larger nuts than American hazel. Plant 2+ varieties for pollination.

name: Serviceberry / Juneberry
notes: Extremely cold hardy — to zone 2. Native. Early spring flowers. Blueberry-like fruit. Birds love it.

name: Aronia — Black Chokeberry
notes: Native. Extremely adaptable — wet or dry soil. Very high antioxidants. Brilliant fall color. Underused gem.

name: Sea Buckthorn
notes: Nitrogen-fixer. Drought and wind hardy. Extremely high vitamin C. Male and female plants — need both. Thorny.

name: Goji Berry / Wolfberry
notes: Drought tolerant once established. Spreads by runners. Prefers alkaline soil. Berries dried or fresh.

name: Comfrey — Bocking 14
notes: Non-seeding variety. Deep taproot mines minerals. Chop-and-drop mulch. Bee magnet. Medicinal topically.

name: Yarrow
notes: Native to North America. Excellent pollinator plant — 100s of insect species. Medicinal wound herb. Spreads happily.

name: Lavender — English
notes: Must have well-drained soil. Incredible bee plant. Deer resistant. Essential oils, culinary, medicinal.

name: Echinacea / Purple Coneflower
notes: Native prairie plant. Drought tolerant once established. Immune-support herb. Goldfinches love the seeds.

name: Valerian
notes: Deep taproot. Attractive to cats. Root used for sleep/anxiety. Butterflies love the flowers.

name: Rosemary
notes: Tender in zones below 7 — treat as annual or container in cold climates. Drought and heat tolerant.

name: Sage — Garden
notes: Drought tolerant once established. Deters cabbage moths. Bees love the flowers in spring.

name: Thyme — Common
notes: Excellent groundcover under fruit trees. Attracts parasitic wasps. Culinary and medicinal.

name: Chives
notes: Deters aphids and carrot fly. Bees love the flowers. Divide clumps every 2–3 years.

name: Spearmint
notes: Spreads aggressively — contain in pots or use as groundcover where spreading is welcome. Deters rodents.

name: Lemon Balm
notes: Bee magnet — bees love it. Calming herb. Spreads vigorously. Great under fruit trees in partial shade.

name: St. John's Wort
notes: Naturalizes well. Bright yellow flowers. Medicinal for mood support — caution with some medications.

name: Black Walnut
notes: Native. Produces juglone — allelopathic to some plants (avoid near apples, tomatoes). Excellent wildlife and timber value.

name: Butternut / White Walnut
notes: Native. More cold hardy than black walnut. At risk from butternut canker disease. Lighter juglone effect.

name: Chestnut — American Hybrid
notes: Blight-resistant hybrid. Plant 2+ for pollination. High yields. Excellent wildlife mast crop. Prefers acidic soil.

name: Pecan
notes: Native to southeastern US. Long-lived. Need 2 varieties (type I and II) for good nut set. Requires long hot summer.

name: White Oak
notes: Keystone native species — supports 500+ caterpillar species. Acorns are mast crop. Long-lived generational tree.

name: Eastern Redbud
notes: Native. Nitrogen-fixer in legume family. One of the earliest spring blooms — critical for early pollinators. Edible flowers.

name: Black Locust
notes: Powerful nitrogen-fixer. Extremely hard, rot-resistant timber. Fragrant flowers great for bees. Can spread aggressively — site carefully.

name: Osage Orange / Hedge Apple
notes: Native to southwest Missouri / Ozarks. Historically used as living fence. Extremely hard wood. Heat, drought, and wind tolerant.

name: American Sycamore
notes: Native to eastern US. Riparian tree — great along creeks. Cavity-nesting wildlife. Distinctive white/mottled bark.

name: Siberian Pea Shrub
notes: Exceptional nitrogen-fixer. Extremely cold and drought hardy. Seeds are edible protein. Excellent windbreak plant.

name: Russian Olive
notes: Nitrogen-fixer. Extremely drought tolerant. Silvery foliage. Edible (though small) fruit. Invasive in some western US states — check locally.

name: Autumn Olive
notes: Prolific nitrogen-fixer. High lycopene fruit. Invasive in some eastern states — check restrictions. Excellent pioneer plant.

name: Groundnut / Hopniss
notes: Native nitrogen-fixer with edible tubers. Used by Native Americans. Climbs on support. Prefers moist areas.

name: Grape — Concord
notes: Classic American grape. Cold hardy. Self-fertile. Needs annual pruning. Juice, jelly, and fresh eating.

name: Grape — Norton / Cynthiana
notes: Native American grape. Disease resistant. The Missouri and Virginia wine grape. Drought tolerant once established.

name: Hardy Kiwi
notes: Grape-sized kiwi eaten skin and all. Need 1 male per 6–8 females. Vigorous — needs strong trellis.

name: Hops
notes: Grows 20ft+ per season. Dies back in winter. Strobiles used in brewing. Sedative medicinal. Deep taproot improves soil.

name: Rhubarb
notes: Perennial — plant once, harvest for decades. Needs cold winters to thrive. Stalks edible; leaves toxic. Classic spring crop.

name: Asparagus
notes: Perennial — harvest for 20+ years. Plant crowns for faster production. Do not harvest first 2 years.

name: Globe Artichoke
notes: Perennial in warm zones. Spectacular architectural plant. Bees love the flowers if left to bloom.

name: Jerusalem Artichoke / Sunchoke
notes: Native. Extremely productive — once planted will come back every year. Can become invasive. Flowers feed pollinators.

name: Sweet Potato
notes: Perennial in zone 8+; annual elsewhere. Covers ground quickly. Drought tolerant once established. Needs long hot season.

name: Garlic — Hardneck
notes: Plant in fall, harvest in summer. Hardneck varieties need cold winters — not for zone 9+. Deters many pests.

name: White Clover
notes: Nitrogen-fixer — naturalizes in pathways and under trees. Excellent bee forage. Tolerates foot traffic.

name: Crimson Clover
notes: Annual nitrogen-fixer. Spectacular red flowers. Excellent cover crop to plow in before planting. Bees love it.

name: Buckwheat
notes: Fast-growing summer cover crop. Smothers weeds. Bees love the white flowers. Edible grain. 70-day cycle.

name: Daikon Radish / Tillage Radish
notes: Roots penetrate compacted soil — natural tillage. Freeze-kills and rots in winter leaving channels for earthworms.

name: Blue Camas
notes: Native. Important food source for indigenous peoples. Spectacular blue flowers in spring. Tolerates wet spring soils.

name: Nanking Cherry
notes: Extremely cold hardy. Small but prolific tart cherries. Plant 2+ for cross-pollination. Great windbreak shrub.

name: Rugosa Rose
notes: Extremely cold and wind hardy. Large rose hips (high vitamin C). Edible petals. Excellent thorny barrier hedge.

name: Spicebush
notes: Native woodland understory shrub. Spicebush swallowtail host plant. Edible berries and leaves (allspice-like). Early spring flowers.

name: American Beautyberry
notes: Native. Spectacular purple berries in fall — birds love them. Natural insect repellent. Shade tolerant.

name: Buttonbush
notes: Native wetland/riparian shrub. Unique white ball flowers — butterflies, bees, hummingbirds. Perfect for pond edges and rain gardens.

name: Bee Balm / Bergamot
notes: Native. Hummingbirds, bees, butterflies. Spreads by rhizomes. Tea herb — bergamot-like flavor. Medicinal.

name: Goldenrod
notes: Native keystone plant — supports 100+ bee species. Late season pollinator support critical for overwinter. Medicinal diuretic.

name: Milkweed — Common
notes: Essential Monarch butterfly host plant. Native. Fragrant flowers. Spreads by rhizome and seed. Drought tolerant once established.

name: Black-Eyed Susan
notes: Native prairie wildflower. Self-seeds freely. Goldfinches eat seeds in winter. Excellent for naturalizing.

name: Wild Bergamot
notes: Native to Ozarks and midwest. More drought tolerant than M. fistulosa. Early bloomer. Native bee magnet.

name: Borage
notes: Annual — self-seeds prolifically once established. Bees adore it. Edible flowers. Dynamic accumulator. Great with tomatoes.

name: Nasturtium
notes: Annual everywhere. Entirely edible — leaves, flowers, seeds. Repels aphids. Self-seeds readily. Trailing or climbing.

name: Sunflower
notes: Annual. Easy and rewarding. Seed heads feed birds through winter. Phytoremediation of contaminated soil.

name: Pole Bean
notes: Annual nitrogen-fixer. Classic Three Sisters partner. High yield over long season. Needs support.

name: Sweet Corn
notes: Annual. Needs warm soil 60°F+. Plant in blocks for wind pollination. Three Sisters companion with beans and squash.

name: Winter Squash / Pumpkin
notes: Annual. Three Sisters groundcover. Stores through winter. Large vines suppress weeds.

name: Tomato — Heirloom
notes: Annual everywhere. Avoid near black walnut. Rich soil, consistent water. Rotate beds annually.

name: Sweet Pepper
notes: Annual. Needs long warm season. Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost. Perennial in frost-free zones.

name: Cucumber
notes: Annual. Fast producer — 50 days to harvest. Loves heat. Train on trellis to save space and improve air circulation.

name: Zucchini / Summer Squash
notes: Annual. Extremely productive — 1–2 plants feeds a family. Harvest young for best flavor. Bees needed for pollination.

name: Hedge / Living Fence Mix
notes: Mix of hawthorn, plum, rose, elderberry, and hazel for a productive, wildlife-rich living fence or windbreak.

name: Linden / Basswood
notes: Native. Incredibly fragrant flowers — major honeybee nectar source. Flowers and young leaves edible. Medicinal flowers.

name: Tulip Poplar / Yellow Poplar
notes: Native. Fast-growing. Major honey bee nectar source. Spectacular tulip-shaped flowers. Important timber.

name: Flowering Dogwood
notes: Native understory tree. Spectacular spring flowers. Red berries critical for migrating birds in fall. Prefers acidic soil.

name: Wild Plum — American
notes: Native. Forms thickets — excellent wildlife cover. Edible tart fruit. Early spring blooms critical for pollinators.

name: Hawthorn — Mayhaw
notes: Native to southeast. Beloved for mayhaw jelly. Tolerates wet feet. Early spring fruit. Dense thorny habitat for birds.

name: Hawthorn — Washington
notes: Native. Dense thorny habitat for birds. Red berries persist into winter. Heart-supportive medicinal berries. Excellent wildlife tree.
